perturbation.original_text: Each party acknowledges that it is aware, and will advise its Representatives who are informed as to the matters which are the subject of this Agreement, that the United States securities laws prohibit any person who has received from an issuer material, non-public information concerning the matters which are the subject of this Agreement from purchasing or selling securities of such issuer or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party acknowledges that it is aware that purchasing or selling securities may have some restrictions.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly states that United States securities laws prohibit insider trading. The modified text weakens this statement, potentially misleading parties about their responsibilities regarding insider trading, violating SEC regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text fails to adequately inform parties of the prohibition against using material, non-public information for securities trading, thereby contradicting Rule 10b-5, which prohibits employing any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, making any untrue statement of a material fact, or omitting to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: SEC.gov | Selective Disclosure and Insider Trading Skip to search field Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock A locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. More in this Section Final Rule Selective Disclosure and Insider Trading Overview SECURITIES AND EXCHANGE COMMISSION 17 CFR Parts 240, 243, and 249 Release Nos. 33-7881, 34-43154, IC-24599, File No. S7-31-99 RIN 3235-AH82 Selective Disclosure and Insider Trading AGENCY: Securities and Exchange Commission. ACTION: Final rule. SUMMARY: The Securities and Exchange Commission is adopting new rules to address three issues: the selective disclosure by issuers of material nonpublic information; when insider trading liability arises in connection with a trader's "use" or "knowing possession" of material nonpublic information; and when the breach of a family or other non-business relationship may give rise to liability under the misappropriation theory of insider trading. The rules are designed to promote the full and fair disclosure of information by issuers, and to clarify and enhance existing prohibitions against insider trading. EFFECTIVE DATE: The new rules and amendments will take effect October 23, 2000. FOR FURTHER INFORMATION CONTACT: Richard A. Levine, Sharon Zamore, or Jacob Lesser, Office of the General Counsel at (202) 942-0890; Amy Starr, Office of Chief Counsel, Division of Corporation Finance at (202) 942-2900. SUPPLEMENTARY INFORMATION: The Securities and Exchange Commission today is adopting new rules: Regulation FD, 1 Rule 10b5-1, 2 and Rule 10b5-2. 3 Additionally, the Commission is adopting amendments to Form 8-K. 4 I. Executive Summary We are adopting new rules and amendments to address the selective disclosure of material nonpublic information by issuers and to clarify two issues under the law of insider trading. In response to the comments we received on the proposal, we have made several modifications, as discussed below, in the final rules. Regulation FD (Fair Disclosure) is a new issuer disclosure rule that addresses selective disclosure. The regulation provides that when an issuer, or person acting on its behalf, discloses material nonpublic information to certain enumerated persons (in general, securities market professionals and holders of the issuer's securities
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly prohibits insider trading based on United States securities laws. The changed text weakens this to merely stating that purchasing or selling securities 'may have some restrictions'. This omission of the explicit prohibition contradicts Rule 10b-5, as it fails to adequately inform parties of the prohibition against using material, non-public information for securities trading, potentially misleading parties about their responsibilities regarding insider trading, which falls under SEC regulations, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 2, which states that the SEC is adopting rules to 'clarify and enhance existing prohibitions against insider trading.'

perturbation.original_text: In the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, if a court of competent jurisdiction determines that either party or any of its Representatives have breached this Agreement, then the breaching party shall be liable and pay to the non-breaching party the reasonable legal fees incurred in connection with such litigation, including an appeal therefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, each party shall be responsible for their own legal fees, regardless of the outcome.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates the breaching party to pay the non-breaching party's legal fees. The modified text removes this obligation, creating a contradiction if Minnesota Statutes mandate fee-shifting in certain contract disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts Minnesota Statutes § 549.211, which allows for the imposition of attorney's fees against a party that acts in bad faith regarding litigation.  By stating that each party is responsible for their own fees 'regardless of the outcome', the contract effectively waives the right to seek attorney's fees even if the opposing party acts in bad faith, asserting frivolous claims or defenses.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 549.211 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes CIVIL PROCEDURE Chapter 549 Section 549.211 549.21 549.23 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 549 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 549.211 Version List Recent History 1997 549.211 New 1997 c 213 art 1 s 1 549.211 SANCTIONS IN CIVIL ACTIONS. § Subdivision 1. Acknowledgment required. The parties by their attorneys in a civil action shall attach to and make a part of the pleading, written motions, and papers served on the opposite party or parties a signed acknowledgment stating that the parties acknowledge that sanctions may be imposed under this section. § Subd. 2. Effect of acknowledgment. By presenting to the court, whether by signing, filing, submitting, or later advocating, a pleading, written motion, or other paper, an attorney or unrepresented party is certifying that to the best of the person's knowledge, information, and belief, formed after an inquiry reasonable under the circumstances: (1) it is not being presented for any improper purpose, such as to harass or to cause unnecessary delay or needless increase in the cost of litigation; (2) the claims, defenses, and other legal contentions are warranted by existing law or by a nonfrivolous argument for the extension, modification, or reversal of existing law or the establishment of new law; (3) the allegations and other factual contentions have evidentiary support or, if specifically so identified, are likely to have evidentiary support after a reasonable opportunity for further investigation or discovery; and (4) the denials of factual contentions are warranted on the evidence or, if specifically so id
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principle outlined in the Law Explanation, which refers to Minnesota Statutes § 549.211. The original contract allows for fee-shifting to the non-breaching party. The changed text states each party is responsible for their own legal fees 'regardless of the outcome'. This nullifies the possibility of fee-shifting even if one party acts in bad faith during litigation, directly conflicting with the potential sanctions described in the provided law snippet, particularly if a party presents a pleading for an improper purpose, as noted in Subdivision 2(1) of Section 549.211.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the internal laws of the State of Minnesota, without giving effect to the principles of conflicts of laws thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the jurisdiction determined at the sole discretion of BBY.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly defines Minnesota law as the governing law. By allowing BBY to choose the jurisdiction, the modified text could allow them to choose a jurisdiction with laws less favorable to the Company, effectively waiving protections provided under Minnesota law and any implied duty of good faith and fair dealing as recognized in Minnesota.
perturbation.law_explanation: The change could potentially violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Minnesota Statutes § 336.1-304, if the choice of jurisdiction is used to undermine the contract's purpose or deprive the other party of the reasonably expected benefits of the agreement. It becomes an issue if the other jurisdiction chosen by BBY does not recognize such a covenant, effectively removing a protection that Musicland Stores Corporation would have had under Minnesota law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 336.1-304 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 336 Section 336.1-304 336.1-303 336.1-305 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 336 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 336.1-304 Version List Recent History 2004 336.1-304 New 2004 c 162 art 1 s 19 336.1-304 OBLIGATION OF GOOD FAITH. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. History: 2004 c 162 art 1 s 19 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows BBY to choose any jurisdiction, potentially one without an implied covenant of good faith. Minnesota Statutes § 336.1-304 states, 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By allowing for a different jurisdiction, the modified text could remove this obligation, creating a legal contradiction by potentially undermining protections Musicland Stores Corporation would have under Minnesota law. The original contract specifies Minnesota law to govern the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: In addition, BBY agrees that during the Restricted Period, BBY shall not employ any Restricted Employee BBY solicited for employment during the six (6) month period immediately preceding the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition, BBY agrees that during the Restricted Period, BBY shall not employ any Restricted Employee.
perturbation.explanation: The original text prevents BBY from hiring employees they solicited in the six months prior to the agreement's date. By removing this qualification, it creates an overly broad restriction and potential contradiction with Minnesota law regarding non-compete agreements, as such broad restrictions may be deemed unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text now introduces an unenforceable non-compete. Minnesota Statutes § 181.98 outlines specific requirements for non-compete agreements, including reasonable geographic and temporal limitations. Removing the solicitation period and banning *any* Restricted Employee makes the non-solicitation clause likely unenforceable under MN ST § 181.98 because it's unreasonably broad.
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the 'solicited for employment during the six (6) month period' restriction, broadening the non-compete clause. According to the perturbation explanation, this creates an overly broad restriction and potential contradiction with Minnesota law. The law explanation states Minnesota Statutes § 181.98 outlines specific requirements for non-compete agreements, including reasonable geographic and temporal limitations, suggesting that the removal of the solicitation period makes the non-solicitation clause likely unenforceable because it's unreasonably broad. Without relevant law snippets, the decision relies on the provided explanations. The original text contained a temporal and scope limit while the changed text has removed it. Based on the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction (removing essential information), the contract is non-compliant, therefore a contradiction exists.
